status.state_description: Waiting for human approval of the plan
status.product_description: AI travel planner with itinerary generation, flight/hotel booking integration, budget tracking, group trip planning, and offline maps.

plan.description: Analyze AI travel planner market: competitors (Google Travel, TripIt, Wanderlog, Hopper), pricing models, user pain points, TAM/SAM/SOM, and go-to-market positioning for travel_planner.
acceptance_criteria: Competitive matrix covers at least 6 competitors across 5 dimensions
Unique differentiators for travel_planner clearly stated
TAM/SAM/SOM estimates with sources
Go-to-market strategy with 3 target personas defined

plan.description: Write Product Requirements Document for travel_planner: user stories for itinerary generation, flight/hotel booking, budget tracking, group trip planning, and offline maps. Define success metrics, MVP scope, and phased roadmap.
acceptance_criteria: User stories written for all 5 core feature areas with acceptance criteria
MVP feature set explicitly scoped (Phase 1 vs Phase 2)
Success metrics defined: DAU, itinerary completion rate, booking conversion, group trip retention
Roadmap covers 3 phases with time estimates
Non-functional requirements documented: offline sync, latency targets, supported locales

plan.description: Conduct user research and produce wireframes for all travel_planner flows: onboarding, AI itinerary chat, booking search/compare, budget dashboard, group invite/vote, and offline map download.
acceptance_criteria: User journey maps for all 3 personas from PRD
Low-fidelity wireframes for all 7 screen areas
Interaction design spec for AI chat interface (streaming responses, suggestion chips)
Group planning flow covers: invite, vote on destinations, split costs
Accessibility audit checklist completed for each wireframe
Wireframes reviewed and approved by product_manager
App store screenshot mockups prepared
Onboarding flow under 3 steps

plan.description: Set up travel_planner solution scaffold: copy starter template, configure project.yaml/prompts.yaml/tasks.yaml for the travel domain, set up agentic pattern config (coordinator pattern for multi-domain agent routing across itinerary, booking, budget, maps subsystems).
acceptance_criteria: solutions/travel_planner/ directory created from starter template
project.yaml has correct solution name, domain, active_modules, and theme block
prompts.yaml defines roles for itinerary, booking, budget, maps, and group coordinator agents
tasks.yaml defines task types: itinerary_generate, flight_search, hotel_search, budget_update, group_invite, map_download
make run PROJECT=travel_planner starts without errors
Coordinator pattern wired: central agent routes sub-tasks to specialist agents

plan.description: Design and implement the travel_planner database schema: users, trips, itineraries, bookings (flights + hotels), budget_items, group_members, offline_map_cache metadata.
acceptance_criteria: ERD diagram produced covering all 10 tables with relationships
All tables have UUID primary keys and created_at/updated_at timestamps
trips table links to users (owner) and group_members (shared access)
budget_items table supports currency field and category enum
offline_map_tiles table stores bounding_box, zoom_levels, download_status, size_bytes
Initial Alembic migration runs cleanly on a fresh PostgreSQL instance
Seed data script populates 2 sample trips for testing

plan.description: Design OpenAPI spec for all travel_planner REST and streaming endpoints: itinerary generation (SSE stream), flight/hotel search, booking confirmation, budget CRUD, group management, and offline map tile download.
acceptance_criteria: OpenAPI spec validates with no errors using swagger-parser
All endpoints have request/response schemas, status codes, and error shapes
SSE streaming endpoints documented with event schema
Auth scheme defined (JWT Bearer) on all protected endpoints
Booking endpoints include idempotency key pattern
Map download endpoint returns async job_id with polling status endpoint

plan.description: Implement the AI itinerary generation service using the coordinator agentic pattern. Central coordinator receives trip parameters, fans out to specialist agents (destination researcher, activity planner, logistics optimizer), collects outputs, and streams a cohesive day-by-day itinerary via SSE.
acceptance_criteria: POST /itinerary/generate accepts: destination, dates, travelers, interests, budget_range
Coordinator decomposes request and dispatches to 3 specialist agents in parallel (wave execution)
SSE stream emits partial itinerary chunks as agents complete
Final itinerary persisted to itinerary_days + itinerary_items tables
All LLM calls logged to audit_log.db with trace_id
Itinerary generation completes in under 15 seconds for a 7-day trip
Handles LLM timeout/retry with graceful SSE error event

plan.description: Implement flight and hotel booking integration layer: adapter interfaces for Amadeus (flights) and Booking.com (hotels) APIs, search aggregation, price comparison, and booking confirmation with idempotency.
acceptance_criteria: BookingAdapter interface defined with search(), book(), cancel() methods
AmadeusAdapter and BookingComAdapter implement BookingAdapter
Flight search returns sorted results with price, duration, stops, and deep-link
Hotel search returns results with price/night, rating, amenities, and cancellation policy
Booking creates a record in flight_bookings or hotel_bookings tables
Duplicate booking requests with same idempotency key return existing booking, not a new one
Provider API errors mapped to unified BookingError with user-friendly messages
Mock adapter provided for testing without live API credentials

plan.description: Implement budget tracking service: expense logging, per-category aggregation, multi-currency conversion (via Exchange Rate API), budget vs. actual comparison, and real-time split calculation for group trips.
acceptance_criteria: POST /trips/{id}/budget/expenses adds expense with amount, currency, category, payer
GET /trips/{id}/budget returns totals by category + overall spent vs. budget
All amounts stored in trip's base currency after conversion; conversion rate logged
Group split endpoint returns per-member owed amounts for all 3 split algorithms
Budget alert triggered when spending exceeds 80% and 100% of budget
Currency conversion cache refreshes rates every 6 hours
Unit tests cover all 3 split algorithms with edge cases (unequal shares, zero balances)

plan.description: Implement group trip planning service: trip sharing via invite links, role-based access (owner/editor/viewer), destination voting with ranked-choice, and group activity preference aggregation.
acceptance_criteria: POST /trips/{id}/group/invite generates time-limited invite token (48h expiry)
Invite accept flow adds user to group_members with correct role
Owner can promote/demote editors; editors cannot modify roles
Viewers see read-only trip; editors can modify itinerary and budget items
POST /trips/{id}/group/vote submits ranked destination preferences
GET /trips/{id}/group/vote/results returns ranked-choice tally with winner
Real-time group notifications via WebSocket when itinerary is edited
Integration test covers full group flow: invite → accept → vote → view results

plan.description: Implement offline map service: tile download job orchestration using OpenStreetMap/Mapbox tile API, bounding-box selection, background download queue, tile storage (local filesystem or S3), and sync status API.
acceptance_criteria: POST /maps/download accepts bounding_box + zoom_levels and returns async job_id
Celery worker downloads tiles in background, updating progress in offline_map_tiles table
GET /maps/status/{job_id} returns download percentage and estimated completion
Downloaded tiles served at GET /maps/tiles/{z}/{x}/{y}.png
Cache eviction policy: LRU, max 2GB per user
Download job is resumable after interruption
Integration test verifies tile download, serve, and delete cycle

plan.description: Set up i18n infrastructure for travel_planner: extraction pipeline, translation config, and initial locale files for English, Spanish, French, Japanese, and Portuguese (top travel app locales).
acceptance_criteria: i18next configured with locale files at web/src/locales/{locale}/translation.json
i18next-parser script extracts all t() calls to en/translation.json baseline
Translation files created for all 5 locales (stub translations acceptable for non-English at this stage)
Date/time formatting uses locale-aware Intl.DateTimeFormat throughout
Currency display uses Intl.NumberFormat with correct locale
Language switcher component added to settings page
RTL layout tested with a placeholder RTL locale config
Top 5 target locales identified
Store listing translated

plan.description: Build the travel_planner React frontend: app shell, routing, auth flow, and all core pages — Home/Trip List, AI Itinerary Chat, Booking Search, Budget Dashboard, Group Planning, and Offline Maps.
acceptance_criteria: All 9 pages render without errors with React Router routes configured
AI itinerary chat page streams SSE responses and renders markdown itinerary incrementally
Flight and hotel search pages show sortable, filterable result lists
Budget dashboard shows category breakdown chart (Recharts) + group split table
Group planning page shows member list, invite modal, and destination voting UI
Offline maps page shows region selector on Mapbox GL JS map + download progress bar
All pages are responsive (mobile 375px, tablet 768px, desktop 1280px)
i18n hook (useTranslation) used for all user-visible strings
Lighthouse accessibility score ≥ 90 on itinerary_chat and budget_dashboard pages

plan.description: Implement the iterative refinement agentic pattern for itinerary personalization: after initial itinerary generation, a refinement agent evaluates user feedback signals (thumbs up/down on activities, manual edits), scores the itinerary against preferences, and regenerates improved versions until quality threshold is met.
acceptance_criteria: Refinement agent reads user feedback signals from itinerary_items ratings
Quality scorer returns a 0-1 score across all 4 dimensions
Refinement loop terminates when overall score ≥ 0.85 or max_iterations reached
Each refinement iteration stored in history with score delta
User can trigger manual refinement via POST /itinerary/{id}/refine
Group consensus dimension aggregates all members' ratings before scoring
Refinement history visible in audit_log.db with trace_ids

plan.description: Perform security review of travel_planner: threat model for booking data (PII, payment), JWT auth hardening, API rate limiting, input validation on all endpoints, and OWASP Top 10 checklist.
acceptance_criteria: Threat model document covers all 5 scoped areas with mitigations
JWT tokens use RS256, 1h expiry, refresh token rotation implemented
Rate limiting applied: 10 req/min on /itinerary/generate, 30 req/min on /flights/search
All user inputs validated with Pydantic schemas; no raw SQL string interpolation
Invite tokens are single-use and invalidated after acceptance
npm audit + pip-audit report produced with no critical/high unresolved vulnerabilities
Offline tile endpoints require authenticated session (no public tile serving)

plan.description: Draft Terms of Service, Privacy Policy, and cookie consent banner for travel_planner. Address GDPR/CCPA compliance for location data, travel PII, and third-party booking integrations.
acceptance_criteria: Privacy Policy covers all 4 data types with retention periods and deletion rights
Third-party data sharing clearly disclosed for Amadeus, Booking.com, Mapbox
GDPR Article 13/14 notice requirements met
CCPA 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' opt-out path described
Cookie consent banner text drafted with category breakdown (essential, analytics, marketing)
Terms of Service covers booking liability limitations and group trip shared responsibility

plan.description: Set up CI/CD pipeline, Docker Compose for local dev, and production deployment config for travel_planner: backend (FastAPI), frontend (Vite), PostgreSQL, Redis (Celery), and Celery worker services.
acceptance_criteria: docker-compose.yml starts all 5 services with a single docker-compose up
GitHub Actions workflow runs lint + test on every PR
Build stage produces Docker images tagged with git SHA
Deploy stage pushes to staging on merge to main; production requires manual approval
Health check endpoints wired to Docker HEALTHCHECK instructions
Environment-specific .env files documented with required variables list
Celery worker auto-scales to 4 concurrent workers in production config

plan.description: Write comprehensive unit and integration tests for all travel_planner backend services: itinerary coordinator, booking adapters, budget split algorithms, group invite flow, and offline map job queue.

payload.mock_strategy: MockBookingAdapter for external APIs; real PostgreSQL via testcontainers

acceptance_criteria: Test suite achieves ≥ 80% line coverage across src/
All 3 budget split algorithms (equal, proportional, custom) have edge-case tests
Booking adapter tests use MockBookingAdapter; integration test verifies real Amadeus sandbox
Group invite flow tested: generate → accept → role check → expiry → duplicate reject
Map download job tested: enqueue → worker pickup → progress update → completion
Itinerary coordinator tested with mocked LLM responses for deterministic assertions
CI pipeline runs full test suite in under 5 minutes

plan.description: Develop QA test plan and execute manual test cases for travel_planner: exploratory testing of AI itinerary chat UX, booking end-to-end flow, group planning edge cases, budget tracking accuracy, and offline map download on slow connections.
acceptance_criteria: QA test plan document covers all 5 feature areas with 5+ test cases each
All P0 test cases executed with pass/fail recorded
Zero P0 bugs open at release gate
Itinerary streaming tested on slow 3G (Chrome DevTools throttle) — no broken UI
Group planning tested with 8 simultaneous members editing — no data conflict
Offline map tested by toggling to airplane mode after download — tiles serve correctly
Bug report template created with severity/priority/reproduce-steps fields

plan.description: Execute end-to-end system tests for travel_planner's critical user journeys using Playwright: full itinerary generation → booking → budget tracking flow, and group trip invite → vote → finalize flow.

journeys: solo_trip: onboard → generate itinerary → search flights → book → track budget
group_trip: create trip → invite 3 members → vote destination → finalize itinerary → split costs
offline: search trip → download map region → toggle offline → navigate map

acceptance_criteria: All 3 Playwright journeys pass on staging environment
Solo trip E2E completes in under 60 seconds (excluding LLM generation time)
Group trip flow handles concurrent member actions without race conditions
Offline map journey verified: tile serve returns 200 from local cache after network disabled
E2E tests run in GitHub Actions on every deploy to staging
Test report with screenshots on failure stored as CI artifact

plan.description: Create user onboarding guide, in-app tooltip copy, and help center articles for travel_planner: getting started, creating your first AI itinerary, inviting a group, tracking budget, and downloading offline maps.
acceptance_criteria: Getting started guide covers all 5 topics with screenshots placeholders
In-app tooltip copy provided for every major UI action (JSON keyed to component IDs)
Onboarding checklist drives new user through: create trip → generate itinerary → invite friend → add expense → download map
Help center articles are under 500 words each with a 'Did this help?' feedback widget spec
Content reviewed for all 5 locales (en complete; others marked for translation)
Technical writer sign-off from product_manager

plan.description: Write developer documentation for travel_planner: architecture overview, local setup guide, API reference (generated from OpenAPI spec), agentic pattern documentation, and solution YAML reference.

payload.api_gen_tool: Redoc / Swagger UI from OpenAPI spec

acceptance_criteria: README.md has quickstart that gets a developer running locally in under 10 commands
Architecture diagram (Mermaid) covers all services and data flows
API reference auto-generated from OpenAPI spec and published at /docs
Agentic pattern doc explains coordinator + iterative_refinement patterns with sequence diagrams
YAML reference documents every field in project.yaml, prompts.yaml, tasks.yaml with examples
Local setup guide tested by a second developer who had not previously set up the project

flaws: Payment processing is completely absent. Steps 8 describes 'booking confirmation' but there is no Stripe/Braintree integration, no payment flow, no PCI DSS scope. You cannot book a flight or hotel without charging a card. This is not a minor gap — the entire booking feature is non-functional without it.

Booking.com's Connectivity API is restricted to licensed travel agencies with commercial agreements. It is not a self-service API. Amadeus Self-Service has limited inventory and sandbox-only access without a commercial agreement. The plan treats both as simple REST adapters; obtaining production access to either could take months of business negotiation and is a potential project-killer.

The product description implies a mobile app ('offline maps', 'app store screenshot mockups' in Step 3). The entire implementation is React web + React Router — not React Native or Flutter. A PWA can approximate offline maps but cannot match native map tile caching on iOS/Android. This is a fundamental product/implementation mismatch that is never resolved.

Celery tile workers write to 'local filesystem + optional S3' (Step 11). In a multi-worker production environment, a tile downloaded by worker A is not accessible to worker B serving the tile endpoint. S3 must be mandatory, not optional. The 2GB LRU per-user cap on local filesystem does not scale beyond a single machine.

Real-time group notifications via WebSocket (Step 10) are never wired into infrastructure. Step 17 (DevOps) defines services as FastAPI + Postgres + Redis + Celery — no WebSocket connection manager, no sticky sessions, no pub/sub fan-out. This breaks group editing UX entirely in multi-worker deployments.

The 15-second SLA for 7-day itinerary generation (Step 7) is unrealistic. Three parallel LLM calls (each 5–15s p50 on current models) plus coordinator overhead, SSE handshake, and DB persistence will routinely exceed 15s. There is no fallback or revised UX contract when this SLA is breached.

Group concurrency is untested at the schema level. Step 10 acceptance criteria require 'no data conflict' with 8 simultaneous editors, but the database schema (Step 5) has no optimistic locking fields (version/etag), no row-level locking strategy, and no CRDT approach. This will produce silent last-write-wins overwrites.

GDPR 'right to erasure' is mentioned in the Privacy Policy (Step 16) but the database schema has no soft-delete columns, no cascade delete strategy, no account deletion endpoint, and no data purge job. The legal document will promise a right the backend cannot honor.

The iterative refinement quality scorer (Step 14, threshold 0.85) uses LLM-as-judge with no calibration baseline. LLM quality scores are non-deterministic; the same itinerary can score 0.78 one run and 0.91 the next. There is no validation that the scorer is accurate, no human-labeled ground truth, and no metric to detect scorer drift.

Budget alerts (Step 9) are mentioned as acceptance criteria but there is no notification delivery system anywhere in the plan — no email service, no push notification infrastructure, no SMS. The alert fires into a void.

Mapbox bulk tile download for offline use likely violates Mapbox Terms of Service Section 3 (restrictions on bulk tile extraction). The plan acknowledges 'OSM fallback' but OSM tile servers also prohibit bulk downloads. Legal clearance for offline tile caching is not addressed in the Legal step (Step 16).

Single-use invite tokens (Step 15) conflict with the 48h expiry window (Step 10). If a user clicks the link, the request fails mid-flight (network drop), and they retry, the token is consumed and they are locked out. No retry-safe token invalidation strategy is defined.
suggestions: Add a dedicated payment step using Stripe with a defined PCI scope. Use Stripe's hosted payment page to stay out of PCI DSS SAQ D. Wire payment_intent_id to the flight_bookings and hotel_bookings tables. Define refund/cancellation flows explicitly.

Replace direct Booking.com/Amadeus integration in MVP with an aggregator that has accessible APIs — Duffel (flights, accessible self-service API with real inventory), HotelBeds, or Agoda Partner API. Document the partnership acquisition path as a project dependency, not a technical task.

Decide on web PWA vs native mobile and commit. If PWA: document its offline map limitations explicitly and use Workbox for service worker tile caching. If native: add React Native + Expo to the stack and replace Mapbox GL JS with react-native-maps or Mapbox React Native SDK.

Make S3 (or equivalent object store) mandatory for tile storage in Step 11. Add a shared Redis key namespace for tile metadata so any worker can serve any tile. Define the storage cost model per user at the expected scale.

Add a WebSocket connection manager (e.g., Redis pub/sub + FastAPI WebSocket) as an explicit infrastructure component in Step 17. Define which service owns connection state and how fan-out works across multiple FastAPI instances.

Replace the 15s itinerary SLA with a streaming progress contract: emit a 'planning started' SSE event immediately, stream partial results as each specialist agent completes, set a 60s hard timeout with a graceful truncation. Remove the wall-clock SLA from acceptance criteria.

Add version integer columns to itinerary_days, itinerary_items, and budget_items. Implement optimistic locking at the API layer: accept If-Match header, reject with 409 on version mismatch. This is the minimal viable concurrency control for group editing.

Add a /users/{id}/delete endpoint in Step 9 or a new GDPR Compliance step that cascades deletion across all tables, revokes tokens, and queues a deletion audit event. This must exist before any GDPR-scoped Privacy Policy is published.

Calibrate the quality scorer in Step 14 with at least 20 human-labeled itinerary examples before setting a hard threshold. Use a confidence interval, not a point estimate. Log raw dimension scores to audit_log.db so scorer drift is detectable.

Add a notification service step (or subsystem within Step 9/10): choose SendGrid for email, Firebase Cloud Messaging for push. Define the notification contract (event types, payload schema, user preferences) before wiring alert triggers.

Add a Legal review of Mapbox and OSM offline tile terms to Step 16. If offline caching is prohibited, the entire Step 11 must pivot to a licensed offline map provider (e.g., Maps.me data, HERE Maps offline SDK) or be descoped to Phase 2.
missing: Payment processing and PCI DSS scope definition — the most critical missing element
Booking API commercial access acquisition plan — this is a business dependency, not a code task
Account deletion and GDPR erasure implementation (endpoint + cascade delete + purge job)
Notification delivery infrastructure (email + push) for budget alerts and group events
Optimistic locking / concurrency control strategy for collaborative group editing
Offline map tile licensing review and storage architecture decision (S3 mandatory, not optional)
WebSocket infrastructure design for real-time group collaboration
Mobile vs. web platform decision and corresponding build pipeline
Error compensation / saga pattern for partial booking failures (flight booked, hotel fails)
Quality scorer calibration and validation methodology for Step 14
Currency conversion failure handling and stale-rate fallback policy
Token retry-safety for invite link acceptance flow
security_risks: Passport numbers stored in the database (referenced in Step 16) require encryption at rest. The schema design (Step 5) specifies no column-level encryption or KMS integration. Storing plaintext passport numbers is a regulatory liability in GDPR/CCPA jurisdictions.

Payment tokens referenced as 'sensitive_data' in Step 15 but with no tokenization architecture. If Stripe is used, the raw card number should never reach the backend. If a custom payment flow is built (which this plan implies), it is PCI DSS in-scope and requires formal audit.

Group invite tokens with 48h expiry and no rate limiting on the accept endpoint enable enumeration attacks. Tokens should be 128-bit random, single-use with atomic compare-and-swap invalidation, and the accept endpoint should have its own rate limit separate from the general API limits.

Offline tile endpoints require auth (Step 15 correctly identifies this), but tile URLs at /maps/tiles/{z}/{x}/{y}.png follow a predictable pattern. Without signed URLs or short-lived session tokens on the tile endpoint, authenticated users can share tile URLs that remain accessible.

The WebSocket endpoint for group notifications (Step 10) is not in the security threat model (Step 15). WebSocket connections bypass standard HTTP middleware for auth; JWT validation must be explicit on the upgrade handshake, not assumed from the HTTP session.

Exchange Rate API (Step 9) is an external dependency with no mention of authentication or SSRF protection. If the API URL is configurable, this is an SSRF vector. If hardcoded, a compromised CDN/DNS cache can manipulate exchange rates.
final_review.summary: This is a thorough and well-structured plan that covers the full product lifecycle from market research to documentation, with credible dependency ordering and specific acceptance criteria. However, it has one fundamental hole that makes the core value proposition non-functional: there is no payment processing anywhere in 22 steps. Itinerary generation + booking search is a free feature; actual booking requires charging money, and this is completely absent. Compounding this, the two named booking providers (Amadeus, Booking.com) require commercial agreements that are non-trivial to obtain — the plan treats them as technical integrations when they are business partnerships. The mobile vs. web ambiguity (app store mockups, offline maps, but React web stack) will cause rework when stakeholders realize the PWA cannot match native offline map UX. The offline tile storage architecture (local filesystem, S3 optional) will fail at multi-worker scale without a mandatory shared object store. Group concurrency is untested at the database level — eight simultaneous editors on unversioned rows will corrupt data silently. These are not polish issues; they require architectural decisions before backend implementation begins. Score 54: the plan's structure and research/design steps are solid, but the backend implementation steps (7–11) are building on unresolved foundations. Resolve payment processing, booking API access strategy, and mobile/web platform decision before proceeding to Step 5.

Group invite tokens with 48h expiry and no rate limiting on the accept endpoint enable enumeration attacks. Tokens should be 128-bit random, single-use with atomic compare-and-swap invalidation, and the accept endpoint should have its own rate limit separate from the general API limits.

Offline tile endpoints require auth (Step 15 correctly identifies this), but tile URLs at /maps/tiles/{z}/{x}/{y}.png follow a predictable pattern. Without signed URLs or short-lived session tokens on the tile endpoint, authenticated users can share tile URLs that remain accessible.

The WebSocket endpoint for group notifications (Step 10) is not in the security threat model (Step 15). WebSocket connections bypass standard HTTP middleware for auth; JWT validation must be explicit on the upgrade handshake, not assumed from the HTTP session.

Exchange Rate API (Step 9) is an external dependency with no mention of authentication or SSRF protection. If the API URL is configurable, this is an SSRF vector. If hardcoded, a compromised CDN/DNS cache can manipulate exchange rates.
history.summary: This is a thorough and well-structured plan that covers the full product lifecycle from market research to documentation, with credible dependency ordering and specific acceptance criteria. However, it has one fundamental hole that makes the core value proposition non-functional: there is no payment processing anywhere in 22 steps. Itinerary generation + booking search is a free feature; actual booking requires charging money, and this is completely absent. Compounding this, the two named booking providers (Amadeus, Booking.com) require commercial agreements that are non-trivial to obtain — the plan treats them as technical integrations when they are business partnerships. The mobile vs. web ambiguity (app store mockups, offline maps, but React web stack) will cause rework when stakeholders realize the PWA cannot match native offline map UX. The offline tile storage architecture (local filesystem, S3 optional) will fail at multi-worker scale without a mandatory shared object store. Group concurrency is untested at the database level — eight simultaneous editors on unversioned rows will corrupt data silently. These are not polish issues; they require architectural decisions before backend implementation begins. Score 54: the plan's structure and research/design steps are solid, but the backend implementation steps (7–11) are building on unresolved foundations. Resolve payment processing, booking API access strategy, and mobile/web platform decision before proceeding to Step 5.
